ai_report.key_takeaway: Security concerns overshadow King of Jordan's US visit, while violence in Pakistan and Nigeria raises alarm over regional stability.
ai_report.summary: Today's headlines reflect a mix of political controversies, security issues, and humanitarian crises across various regions. Melania Trump calls for action against Jimmy Kimmel's remarks, while Trump reassures safety for King Abdullah II's upcoming visit amid security threats. In Pakistan, airstrikes have resulted in casualties, and Nigeria faces a kidnapping crisis at an orphanage. Colombia offers a substantial reward for a rebel leader linked to a deadly bombing, and violence in Chad has led to significant fatalities over a water dispute. Additionally, Russian forces are withdrawing from Mali, and a tragic train crash in Indonesia has claimed lives. Meanwhile, the French coastguard continues to rescue migrants in the Channel, highlighting ongoing humanitarian challenges.

Donald Trump’s fate is to be frustrated by monetary policy.

Even assuming he gets his way and Kevin Warsh succeeds Jerome Powell as chair of the Federal Reserve next month, it is unlikely that the president will finally gain control of the Fed.

items.title: West takeaways: Rockets survive Game 4 to keep season alive
items.summary: Here's what has stood out from the latest playoff games of the Western Conference's first round.

items.title: Minus Durant, Rockets stay alive with Game 4 win
items.summary: Amen Thompson scored 23 points, Tari Eason added 20 and the Rockets, playing without Kevin Durant, avoided elimination with a 115-96 rout of the Lakers in Game 4 of the Western Conference first-round playoff series Sunday night.

items.title: Wemby back, unhappy with concussion process
items.summary: Victor Wembanyama had 27 points, 12 rebounds and seven blocks in his return from a concussion as the Spurs beat the Blazers for a 3-1 series lead.

items.title: Tomlin: Exit partly rooted in recent playoff woes
items.summary: Mike Tomlin told NBC's Maria Taylor that he walked away, in part, because of the recent lack of playoff success and a belief that some of his veterans were "worthy of the excitement and the optimism associated with new leadership."

items.title: Source: Pavia accepts invite to Ravens minicamp
items.summary: Undrafted Vanderbilt quarterback Diego Pavia, one of the most polarizing figures in college football, accepted an invitation to attend next weekend's rookie minicamp with the Baltimore Ravens, a source told ESPN's Adam Schefter on Sunday afternoon.

items.title: Oilers on brink after officials OK Ducks' OT goal
items.summary: Ryan Poehling scored 2:29 into overtime -- a goal that required a lengthy review -- and the Anaheim Ducks pushed Connor McDavid and the Edmonton Oilers to the brink of first-round elimination with a 4-3 victory in Game 4 on Sunday night at Honda Center.

items.title: Korda goes wire-to-wire for 3rd major, again No. 1
items.summary: Nelly Korda went wire-to-wire in capturing the Chevron Championship -- her third major title -- as she reclaimed the No. 1 spot in women's golf.

items.title: Kopitar's 'extremely special' career ends in loss
items.summary: After announcing his retirement in September, Kings captain Anze Kopitar, the Slovenian center who is the top scorer in franchise history and a two-time Selke Trophy winner, saw his 20-year career close with Los Angeles' 5-1 loss to Colorado on Sunday.

items.title: Swayman outburst caps 'unacceptable' Bruins loss
items.summary: The Bruins' bench couldn't even look Jeremy Swayman in the eye following the goalie's outburst after being pulled in the third period of Game 4 with Boston down 6-0. "We should all be embarrassed," said coach Marco Sturm, his team now facing elimination.

items.title: 📸 NFL draft caps wild week in Pittsburgh sports
items.summary: From the Pirates to the Penguins to the NFL draft, last week was a special one for the city of Pittsburgh.

items.title: Kiper's grades for all 32 NFL draft classes: Why the Cowboys and Jets aced their picks

Meanwhile, Nelly Korda triumphed at the Chevron Championship, marking her third major win and reclaiming the top spot in women's golf. In the NHL, the Anaheim Ducks pushed the Edmonton Oilers to the brink of elimination with a controversial overtime goal, while the Boston Bruins faced embarrassment after a heavy playoff loss. Additionally, Mike Tomlin discussed his departure from the Steelers, citing playoff struggles as a factor, and undrafted quarterback Diego Pavia accepted an invitation to the Ravens' minicamp. Anze Kopitar's illustrious career ended with a loss, and the NFL draft generated significant buzz in Pittsburgh sports.

items.title: Students build a “cosmic radio” to listen for dark matter
items.summary: A group of undergraduate students pulled off something remarkable: they built their own dark matter detector and used it to probe one of physics’ biggest mysteries. Working with limited resources but plenty of creativity, they designed a stripped-down experiment to hunt for axions — hypothetical particles that could make up dark matter.

items.title: Scientists finally solve mystery of strange “golden orb” found 2 miles deep
items.summary: A mysterious “golden orb” found more than two miles deep in the Gulf of Alaska left scientists baffled for over two years, sparking wild speculation about its origins. After an intensive investigation combining deep-sea expertise, microscopic analysis, and advanced DNA sequencing, researchers finally cracked the case. The strange object turned out not to be an egg, sponge, or anything alien, but the remains of tissue from a giant deep-sea anemone.

items.title: This tiny mammal survived the dinosaur apocalypse and changed life on Earth
items.summary: A newly discovered prehistoric mammal may hold clues to how life survived the dinosaur-killing extinction. The tiny species, Cimolodon desosai, lived 75 million years ago and had traits—like a small body and varied diet—that likely boosted survival odds. Found in Baja California, the fossil includes rare skeletal remains that reveal how it moved and lived. Researchers believe its lineage helped mammals endure one of Earth’s deadliest events.

items.title: Scientists just captured a mysterious quantum “dance” inside superconductors
items.summary: In a breakthrough experiment, scientists directly imaged how particles pair up in a system that mimics superconductors. Instead of behaving independently, the pairs moved in a synchronized, dance-like pattern—something never predicted before. This suggests a major gap in the classic theory of superconductivity.

items.title: Scientists may have found the brain’s switch for chronic pain
items.summary: Deep within the brain, scientists have uncovered a hidden “switch” that may decide whether pain fades away—or lingers for months or even years. Researchers found that a small, little-known region called the caudal granular insular cortex (CGIC) acts like a command center, telling the body to keep pain signals alive long after an injury has healed. In animal studies, shutting down this pathway not only prevented chronic pain from forming but could even erase it once it had taken hold.

items.title: Pesticide exposure linked to 150% higher cancer risk in major study
items.summary: A major new study finds that living in pesticide-heavy environments could raise cancer risk by up to 150%, even when the chemicals are considered “safe” on their own. The research suggests these mixtures may silently damage cells years before cancer appears.

items.title: The shocking origin of human eyes traces back to an ancient “cyclops”
items.summary: A bizarre, cyclops-like creature from nearly 600 million years ago may hold the key to how your eyes—and even your sleep cycle—evolved. Scientists have discovered that all vertebrates, including humans, trace their vision back to a single light-sensitive “median eye” perched atop a worm-like ancestor’s head. As this ancient animal shifted from a sedentary to a more active lifestyle, it lost and then reinvented its vision, eventually giving rise to the paired, image-forming eyes we rely on today.

items.title: Fish oil may be hurting your brain, new study finds
items.summary: Fish oil has long been praised as brain-boosting, but new research suggests the story may be more complicated. Scientists found that in people with repeated mild head injuries, a key omega-3 fatty acid in fish oil—EPA—may actually interfere with the brain’s ability to repair itself. Instead of helping recovery, it appears to weaken blood vessel stability, disrupt healing signals, and even contribute to harmful protein buildup linked to cognitive decline.

items.title: Blood vessels found in T. rex bones are rewriting dinosaur science
items.summary: Dinosaur DNA may still be out of reach, but scientists are uncovering something almost as exciting—ancient blood vessels hidden inside fossilized bones. In a massive Tyrannosaurus rex nicknamed Scotty, researchers discovered a network of preserved vessels within a rib that once fractured and began healing 66 million years ago. Using powerful synchrotron X-rays from particle accelerators, they were able to peer inside the dense fossil without damaging it, revealing intricate, iron-rich structures

items.title: DNA research just rewrote the origin of human species
items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new picture of human origins that challenges the long-held idea of a single ancestral population in Africa. By analyzing genetic data from diverse modern African groups—especially the highly distinct Nama people—and comparing it with fossil evidence, researchers found that early humans likely evolved from multiple intermingling populations over hundreds of thousands of years. Rather than a clean split, these groups stayed connected, exchanging genes even af

items.title: Mezcal worm in a bottle DNA test reveals a surprise
items.summary: The famous mezcal “worm” has long puzzled scientists, but DNA testing has finally cracked the case. Researchers found that all sampled larvae were actually agave redworm moth caterpillars—not a mix of species as once believed. While the discovery clears up a long-standing mystery, it also raises concerns about sustainability. Growing demand for mezcal and edible larvae could put pressure on wild populations and the agave plants they depend on.

items.title: Harvard scientists link gut bacteria to depression through hidden inflammation trigger
items.summary: A gut bacterium may be quietly fueling depression through an unexpected chemical twist. Researchers found that when Morganella morganii interacts with a common pollutant, it produces a molecule that triggers inflammation—something strongly linked to depression. This finding helps explain how gut microbes can influence brain health at a molecular level. It also raises the possibility of new treatments that target the immune system rather than just the brain.

Researchers are also uncovering the complexities of chronic pain, the effects of pesticides on cancer risk, and the implications of gut bacteria on mental health.
